Vector database operations: How would yo: Choose an index type (HNSW for low-latency approximate search, IVF for larger scale with a recall/speed tradeoff) sized for expected vector count and query rate, and reindex without downtime by building the new index alongside the old one (blue-green) and cutting over traffic once the new index passes recall/latency validation. Back up the underlying vector store and metadata together so a restore reconstructs a consistent state, and scale query latency with read replicas, sharding by tenant/namespace, and caching frequent queries.
Prompt lifecycle management: How would y: Store prompts in version control alongside application code, not as untracked strings, and require PR review for changes with a diff showing exactly what wording changed. Run an automated evaluation suite (a fixed set of test inputs with expected quality criteria) before merging, deploy behind a feature flag or canary so a new prompt reaches a small percentage of traffic first, and keep the previous prompt version one config change away for instant rollback if quality regresses.
LLM latency debugging: A chat feature ha: Instrument each stage with its own timing span using distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) - retrieval/embedding lookup, model inference (time to first token and total generation time separately), and network round trips - so the trace clearly shows which stage dominates p95 rather than only seeing total request time. Check whether latency is inference-bound (model size, batch contention, cold starts) versus retrieval-bound (vector DB query time, too many retrieved chunks) versus client-side (slow streaming render), since each has a different fix.
Model routing and fallback: How would yo: Build a routing layer that sets a strict timeout on the primary call, catches rate-limit (429) and timeout errors specifically, and retries against a secondary provider/model rather than failing the request outright. For quality-based fallback (not just errors), define a confidence signal (e.g. a low-quality flag from an evaluator model or a heuristic like an empty/truncated response) and route to the secondary on that signal too, logging which model actually served each response for cost and quality tracking.
Streaming inference infra: How would you: Use Server-Sent Events or chunked HTTP streaming with a load balancer and timeout configuration that supports long-lived connections (increase backend timeout well beyond typical generation time, and disable buffering on any proxy in the path). Design the client to handle reconnects by resuming from the last received token offset where possible, and size GKE/Cloud Run concurrency limits around how many simultaneous streaming connections a pod can hold in memory, not just CPU/request-count based autoscaling.
GPU serving cost: How would you decide b: Use managed endpoints (Vertex AI, or a hosted API) for low/unpredictable volume where operational simplicity outweighs per-token cost, and move to self-hosted GPU serving with vLLM/TGI once volume is high and steady enough that GPU utilization amortizes the operational overhead - vLLM's continuous batching in particular improves throughput significantly for high-concurrency workloads. Use batch inference (no real-time latency requirement) with spot/preemptible GPUs for offline scoring jobs, since that's the cheapest option when synchronous response time doesn't matter.
RAG vs fine-tuning vs prompting: How wou: Start with prompt engineering for anything that can be solved with better instructions and few-shot examples, since it has the lowest operational cost and fastest iteration. Add RAG when the model needs access to specific, frequently-changing, or proprietary knowledge it wasn't trained on. Reserve fine-tuning for cases needing a consistent style/format at scale or behavior that prompting genuinely cannot achieve, since fine-tuning adds real operational ownership - training pipelines, versioned model artifacts, evaluation before each retrain, and a rollback story that prompting and RAG don't require.
PII and data handling: How would you pre: Run PII detection/redaction (regex plus a lightweight NER model) on user input before it enters the prompt or gets logged, and configure logging to store redacted prompts by default with raw prompts available only under stricter access control if needed for debugging. Review data processing agreements with third-party model providers before sending any sensitive data, and scrub or synthesize evaluation datasets so real customer PII never ends up in a shared eval/test set.
Vertex AI Agent Builder: How would you o: Version agent configurations (grounding sources, tools, system instructions) in source control the same as application code, and deploy through a staged pipeline (dev -> staging -> prod) with evaluation gates rather than editing the live agent directly. Monitor grounding accuracy, tool-call success rate, and latency/cost per conversation, and keep the previous agent version deployable so a regression in groundedness or tool behavior can be rolled back immediately rather than debugged live in production.
Vertex AI platform design: How would you: Design the platform around repeatable pipelines: data validation and feature preparation, custom training or AutoML, evaluation gates, registration in Vertex AI Model Registry, and controlled deployment to endpoints or batch prediction. Wrap it with least-privilege service accounts, Artifact Registry, Cloud Build or GitHub Actions, model monitoring, audit logs, labels, budgets, and quotas so each model has clear lineage, ownership, rollback, and cost attribution.
Vertex AI custom training: A training jo: Start with Cloud Logging for the custom job and identify whether the failure is import/package related, container startup, permission, data path, or runtime resource exhaustion. Reproduce the container locally with the same entrypoint, verify all dependencies are included, confirm the Vertex AI service account has access to Cloud Storage/BigQuery/Artifact Registry, check region and URI correctness, and add explicit startup validation so packaging and data-access failures happen early.
Vertex AI endpoint latency: A deployed m: Break latency into model inference time, request serialization, network time, cold starts/model loading, and any downstream calls. Check endpoint metrics for replica count, CPU/GPU utilization, queueing, and autoscaling lag; compare machine types and accelerator choices; inspect payload size and preprocessing cost; and load test with realistic concurrency before tuning min replicas, max replicas, batching, model format, or moving heavy preprocessing out of the online path.
Vertex AI model registry: How would you: Register every approved model version with metadata such as training data reference, code commit, pipeline run, metrics, owner, and approval status. Use aliases or deployment metadata to identify staging/candidate/production versions, gate promotion on evaluation thresholds and review, and keep previous production versions available so rollback means redeploying or retargeting the endpoint to a known-good version with full audit history.
Vertex AI batch prediction: When would y: Choose Vertex AI batch prediction when you need managed, large-scale offline scoring with no real-time latency requirement and the model is already in Vertex AI. Use online endpoints for synchronous user-facing predictions, Dataflow when prediction is part of a streaming or complex data transform, Cloud Run jobs for simpler containerized batch tasks, and GKE jobs when you need custom scheduling, GPUs, sidecars, or deeper platform control.
Vertex AI Feature Store: How would you d: Define ownership and SLAs for each feature, including freshness, allowed lag, and backfill behavior. Keep offline training features and online serving features generated from the same transformation logic where possible, track lineage back to source tables and pipeline runs, enforce access with IAM and data governance policies, and monitor missing values, distribution changes, stale features, and training-serving skew.
Vertex AI model monitoring: What would y: Monitor input schema validity, missing/null rates, feature distribution drift against the training baseline, training-serving skew, prediction distribution shifts, and business or label-based quality metrics when ground truth arrives. Tune thresholds with minimum sample sizes and persistence windows, route only actionable alerts to on-call, and use dashboards or tickets for lower-severity drift so monitoring improves reliability without creating noisy pages.
Vertex AI CI/CD: How would you build a C: Use CI to lint/test training and pipeline code, build and scan containers, push images to Artifact Registry, and compile the Vertex AI Pipeline spec. The CD path runs the pipeline in a controlled environment, evaluates metrics against thresholds, registers only passing models, and promotes to staging/production through approvals or automated canary checks, with rollback handled by redeploying the previous registry version.
Vertex AI security: How would you secure: Give each pipeline, training job, and endpoint a dedicated least-privilege service account rather than broad project roles, and avoid long-lived keys. Use private networking where supported, restrict data access with VPC Service Controls for sensitive projects, use CMEK when encryption-key ownership is required, keep Artifact Registry and data stores locked down, and rely on Cloud Audit Logs plus labels to trace who trained, registered, and deployed each model.
Vertex AI cost optimization: A Vertex AI: Start with billing export by project, label, model, endpoint, training job, region, and SKU to identify whether spend is coming from training, online prediction, batch jobs, GPUs, idle replicas, or logging. Reduce waste by right-sizing machines, setting endpoint min replicas appropriately, scheduling non-prod shutdowns, using batch prediction for offline workloads, caching or batching requests, pruning unused models/endpoints, and comparing cheaper model sizes against quality metrics before changing production.
MLflow tracking design: How would you st: Create experiments around stable business problems or model families, not every individual run, and use consistent run names that include project, branch or pipeline run, and purpose. Log hyperparameters as params, evaluation results as metrics, model files and reports as artifacts, and use tags for owner, git commit, dataset version, environment, feature set, and candidate/prod status so runs are searchable and comparable across the team.
MLflow model registry: How would you use: Register only evaluated candidate models, attach metadata linking the model version to the MLflow run, git commit, dataset, metrics, and owner, and require an approval step before promotion. Use stages or aliases to identify candidate, staging, and production versions, keep older production versions available for rollback, and record comments or approvals in the registry so model promotion is auditable rather than a silent artifact overwrite.
MLflow reproducibility: What exactly wou: Log the code version, dataset snapshot or query/version, feature transformation version, parameters, metrics, random seed, library/environment file, training container image, model artifact, evaluation report, and any preprocessing artifacts. The goal is that someone can reconstruct not just the trained model file but the exact training context that produced it, which is essential for debugging, audit, retraining, and rollback.
MLflow artifact storage: How would you d: Run the MLflow tracking server stateless, store metadata in a managed database such as Cloud SQL/Postgres, and store artifacts in durable object storage such as GCS or S3 rather than local disk. Protect it with SSO or gateway auth, restrict bucket/database access with least privilege, back up the backend store, define retention for old runs/artifacts, and monitor server latency, database health, storage growth, and failed artifact uploads.
MLflow CI/CD integration: How would you: Have CI test the training code and build the training/serving image, then run training in a controlled job that logs metrics and artifacts to MLflow with the git SHA attached. A promotion step compares metrics against thresholds, registers a model version only if it passes, and triggers deployment to staging or production through an approval or canary gate, with rollback pointing the serving layer back to the previous registry version.
MLflow on Kubernetes: How would you depl: Deploy MLflow as a stateless Kubernetes Deployment, use Cloud SQL/Postgres for the backend store, GCS for artifacts, Workload Identity for bucket/database access where possible, and an ingress or internal load balancer protected by SSO/IAP or an auth proxy. Add resource requests/limits, health checks, TLS, backups, audit logging, dashboards for request errors and latency, and alerts for database or artifact-store failures.
MLflow vs Vertex AI: When would you choo: Choose MLflow when you want open-source portability, framework-neutral tracking, and a registry that can work across clouds or self-hosted Kubernetes. Choose Vertex AI native tracking and registry when the platform is strongly GCP-managed and you want tight integration with Vertex AI Pipelines, endpoints, monitoring, IAM, and audit logs. Many teams use both: MLflow for experiment tracking and collaboration, then publish approved models into Vertex AI for managed deployment and monitoring.
MLflow model serving: How would you depl: Package the MLflow model with its dependencies and signature, then choose a serving path based on control needs: MLflow's built-in serving for simple tests, a Docker/FastAPI wrapper for custom preprocessing and APIs, KServe/Seldon on Kubernetes for production traffic management, or a managed endpoint for lower operations overhead. Validate input schema, add health checks, observability, canary rollout, and a rollback path to the previous registry version.
MLflow governance: How would you enforce: Use required tags and registry metadata for owner, data source, git commit, pipeline run, risk tier, approval status, and deployment target, then enforce promotion through automation rather than manual UI changes. Store evaluation reports, bias/explainability checks, vulnerability scan evidence, and approval comments as artifacts or registry notes so the model's path from experiment to production is traceable for audit and incident review.
MLflow troubleshooting: An MLflow run lo: Check whether the run completed the artifact logging step, inspect the artifact URI, verify the tracking server and client are using the same artifact store configuration, and confirm the service account or user has write/read permissions to the bucket or filesystem. Then verify dependency/environment files, model flavor, serialization format, and MLflow version compatibility, because a model can exist in storage but still fail to load if the environment or flavor metadata is wrong.
Kubeflow platform design: How would you: Run Kubeflow on a dedicated GKE platform with separate namespaces/profiles per team, GPU and CPU node pools, Workload Identity, Artifact Registry, Cloud Storage, and centralized logging/monitoring. Provide managed notebook access, Kubeflow Pipelines for repeatable workflows, training operators for distributed jobs, KServe for model serving, and platform guardrails such as RBAC, quotas, network policies, image scanning, backups, and clear ownership for each component.
Kubeflow Pipelines: How would you design: Model the workflow as containerized components connected by typed inputs/outputs: validate data, transform features, train, evaluate, and only register or deploy if metrics pass thresholds. Store artifacts in object storage, record metadata for lineage, parameterize environment-specific values, enable caching only when cache keys include data/code/config versions, and keep deployment as a conditional step so bad models stop before production.
Kubeflow components: How would you packa: Package each component as a small container with a clear command, pinned dependencies, and explicit input/output contracts. Pass lightweight values as parameters and larger files/models/reports as artifacts in shared storage, version component images in Artifact Registry, document expected schemas, and avoid embedding project-specific paths so the same component can be reused across teams and environments.
Kubeflow multi-user isolation: How would: Give each team a Kubeflow profile mapped to its own namespace, service accounts, resource quotas, and role bindings. Restrict cross-namespace access with RBAC and network policies, use Workload Identity for least-privilege GCP access, apply separate CPU/GPU quotas to prevent noisy neighbors, and tag/label workloads so usage, cost, and ownership are visible.
Kubeflow distributed training: How would: Use Kubeflow Training Operators such as TFJob or PyTorchJob, schedule workers on GPU node pools with node selectors, taints/tolerations, and resource requests, and store checkpoints in durable storage so failed jobs can resume. Monitor GPU utilization, logs, and job status, set sensible restart policies and backoff limits, and validate that distributed networking, image dependencies, and data access work before scaling to expensive multi-GPU runs.
Kubeflow Katib: When would you use Katib: Use Katib when manual tuning is too slow and the model has a clear objective metric such as validation accuracy, F1, or loss. Limit cost by bounding the search space, max trials, parallel trials, GPU requests, and experiment duration; expose metrics from the training job consistently; and use early stopping so weak trials release resources quickly instead of burning GPU time.
Kubeflow model serving: How would you se: Use KServe InferenceService resources to deploy model servers with explicit model artifact URIs, resource requests, autoscaling settings, and health checks. Roll out new versions with canary traffic splits or separate revisions, monitor latency, error rate, model quality, and GPU utilization, and keep the previous model artifact and serving config available so rollback is a quick traffic shift or manifest revert.
Kubeflow CI/CD: How would you integrate: Use CI to test component code, build and scan container images, push them to Artifact Registry, and compile/version the pipeline spec. Use CD or GitOps to promote pipeline definitions and serving manifests through environments, trigger pipeline runs with approved parameters, gate promotion on evaluation results, and let ArgoCD manage long-lived Kubernetes resources such as KServe services and platform config.
Kubeflow troubleshooting: A Kubeflow pip: Start with the pipeline UI to identify the failed component, then inspect the underlying pod with kubectl: events, scheduling reason, image pull errors, volume mounts, service account permissions, resource requests, node selectors, and logs. Pending often means quota, node capacity, taints/tolerations, PVC, or GPU availability; runtime failure usually points to container command, dependency, data path, or permission issues.
Kubeflow vs MLflow vs Vertex AI: How wou: Use Kubeflow when you want Kubernetes-native control over pipelines, training, serving, and multi-user ML workflows, especially on GKE. Use MLflow when experiment tracking, model packaging, and registry portability are the primary needs. Use Vertex AI when managed GCP operations, IAM/audit integration, managed endpoints, and lower platform maintenance matter most; many real platforms combine them, for example Kubeflow for pipelines, MLflow for tracking, and Vertex AI for managed serving.
MLOps architecture: How would you design: Start with versioned data ingestion, validation, feature engineering, training, experiment tracking, evaluation, model registry, deployment, monitoring, and retraining. Each stage should capture lineage, code version, data version, artifacts, metrics, owner, and approval status, with CI/CD automation, IaC, least-privilege IAM, observability, rollback, and cost controls wrapped around the whole lifecycle.
MLOps lifecycle ownership: Where do data: Data scientists usually own experimentation, feature hypotheses, and model quality; ML engineers productionize training, packaging, evaluation, and serving; platform engineers provide reusable pipelines, infrastructure, registries, and deployment patterns; SREs own reliability practices such as SLOs, monitoring, incident response, and rollback; security owns IAM, secrets, supply chain, privacy, and compliance controls. The handoffs must be explicit so production issues do not fall between teams.
MLOps CI/CD/CT: How would you design con: CI should test code, data contracts, pipeline components, containers, and model-serving APIs. CD should promote versioned artifacts through staging and production with evaluation gates, approvals, canaries, and rollback. CT should retrain from scheduled or trigger-based signals, but promotion still needs validation against baseline/champion metrics so automated retraining cannot silently degrade production.
MLOps data validation: What data quality: Validate schema, required columns, types, ranges, null rates, duplicate records, category drift, feature freshness, volume anomalies, and referential integrity before training or inference. For production, fail fast on hard contract breaks, warn or quarantine suspicious batches, and log validation results as pipeline artifacts so bad data is caught before it creates a bad model or bad predictions.
MLOps model evaluation gates: What offli: Offline gates include accuracy, precision/recall, calibration, regression metrics, robustness, and comparison against the current champion. Online gates include canary or shadow results, latency, error rate, resource usage, and business KPIs. For responsible production use, also check fairness, explainability, data coverage, security, and rollback readiness before allowing promotion.
MLOps monitoring strategy: How would you: Combine service metrics such as latency, throughput, error rate, and resource usage with ML-specific signals such as feature distributions, prediction distributions, data drift, training-serving skew, delayed ground-truth quality metrics, and business KPIs. Use thresholds with minimum sample sizes and persistence windows, route severe user-impact issues to paging, and send lower-severity drift to review workflows to avoid alert fatigue.
MLOps retraining strategy: How would you: Use scheduled retraining when the business has predictable data changes, trigger-based retraining when drift or performance degradation is measurable, and manual retraining for high-risk models where human review is required. Champion-challenger works well when a candidate can be evaluated safely against the current model; in all cases, retraining should produce a candidate, not automatically overwrite production without evaluation gates.
MLOps incident response: A production mo: Treat it as an ML incident: confirm impact with prediction samples and business metrics, compare current inputs against training baselines, check feature freshness, upstream data pipelines, schema changes, model version, recent deployments, and ground-truth feedback. Mitigate by rolling back to the previous model, disabling risky features, routing to rules/manual review, or reducing traffic while preserving logs and artifacts for root-cause analysis.
MLOps governance: How would you make mod: Require every production model to have lineage linking data, code, pipeline run, parameters, metrics, artifacts, approvals, and deployment history. Add model cards or documentation, explainability and fairness checks where relevant, least-privilege access, signed/scanned containers, audit logs, retention policies, and approval workflows so governance is built into the pipeline rather than handled manually after deployment.
MLOps cost and reliability: How would yo: Classify workloads by latency and criticality: use batch prediction for offline work, online endpoints only when users need synchronous responses, and GPUs only when CPU or smaller models cannot meet SLOs. Right-size accelerators, use autoscaling and quotas, monitor utilization and cost by model/team, and keep rollback simple through versioned model artifacts and canary deployments so reliability does not require uncontrolled overprovisioning.
MLOps situation - bad model release: A n: First stabilize: pause the rollout, reduce or shift traffic back to the previous model, and confirm the KPI drop is real rather than a dashboard or attribution delay. Then compare canary segments, prediction distributions, feature values, model version, and recent upstream data changes. Preserve evidence, communicate impact and mitigation, and after recovery add stronger online gates, shadow testing, or business-metric canaries so offline metrics are not the only release signal.
MLOps situation - data pipeline delay: T: Check whether the retraining job requires the delayed data for correctness or whether older validated data is acceptable under the model's freshness SLA. If freshness is critical, skip or hold the run and alert the data owner; if the model is robust to lag, run with the last complete validated snapshot and clearly tag the data version. The key is to avoid silently training on partial data and to make freshness checks part of the pipeline gate.
MLOps situation - silent schema change:: Detect it through distribution checks, feature importance changes, business KPI shifts, training-serving skew checks, and contract tests that validate semantics where possible, not just column names. Mitigate by rolling back or disabling the affected feature, retraining only after data is corrected, and notifying the upstream owner. Prevent it with data contracts, ownership, change review, feature documentation, and alerts on statistical shifts even when schema technically remains valid.
MLOps situation - feature drift alert: D: Treat drift as a signal for investigation, not automatic rollback. Check whether the drift matches an expected business event, compare model quality or proxy metrics by segment, and verify error rates, confidence, and downstream outcomes. If business impact is positive and quality signals are stable, downgrade to watch mode while documenting the event; if sensitive segments degrade, limit traffic or add guardrails for those segments.
MLOps situation - model serving outage:: Check deployment events, pod logs, readiness failures, image pulls, resource limits, model load time, shared dependency limits, database or feature-store connections, and whether all replicas can access the model artifact. Large scale-ups often expose bottlenecks in startup, artifact download, file descriptors, connection pools, or quota. Mitigate by rolling back scale, increasing min ready time, preloading artifacts, tuning dependencies, or adding backpressure.
MLOps situation - GPU quota shortage: A: Immediately check for idle or lower-priority GPU jobs to pause, move the job to another region/machine type if compatible, reduce parallelism, or run a smaller emergency training job. Long term, add GPU quota monitoring, priority classes, team budgets, reservations for critical workloads, spot/preemptible pools for non-critical experiments, and scheduling policies so important jobs are not blocked by ad hoc experimentation.
MLOps situation - model artifact corrupt: Recover by redeploying the previous known-good model version and verifying the artifact URI, checksum, permissions, and environment compatibility for the broken version. Prevent recurrence by making registration atomic, storing checksums, running a load test before promotion, keeping artifacts immutable, scanning/validating model packages, and preventing manual overwrites of production artifact paths.
MLOps situation - rollback conflict: Pro: Frame the decision around agreed release gates and user/business risk, not opinion. Show the latency gain, accuracy drop, affected segments, confidence interval, blast radius, rollback time, and alternatives such as partial rollout or feature flagging. If the model violates an approved guardrail, recommend rollback or limited traffic while a fix is prepared, and document the decision with product, risk, and engineering ownership.
MLOps situation - missing ground truth:: Use proxy signals such as prediction distribution, confidence scores, input drift, business funnel metrics, manual review samples, user feedback, and rule-based anomaly checks until labels arrive. Build a delayed-label evaluation job that backfills true quality metrics after 30 days, compares against the champion model, and updates dashboards. Be explicit that proxies do not replace ground truth; they are early-warning indicators.
MLOps situation - training-serving skew:: Log the exact online features used for predictions and replay a sample through the offline pipeline to compare values, transformations, defaults, encodings, timestamps, and missing-value handling. Check whether training and serving use the same feature code and data sources, and compare distributions by feature. If skew is found, fix the shared transformation/feature store path and add automated parity tests before deployment.
MLOps situation - PII leak risk: You dis: First contain exposure by disabling or redacting the risky logs, restricting access, and preserving evidence for security review. Identify affected systems, time range, data types, and users with access, then involve security/privacy teams for incident classification and notification requirements. Prevent recurrence with PII detection, redaction by default, least-privilege log access, retention limits, and tests that fail builds if sensitive fields are logged.
MLOps situation - expensive experiment c: Start with visibility: labels, dashboards, per-team budgets, and cost by experiment/model. Then add soft guardrails such as default smaller machines, max runtime, quota warnings, spot/preemptible pools, approval for very large jobs, and reusable templates that make efficient choices easy. Keep a path for justified large experiments, but require ownership, expected value, and cleanup.
MLOps situation - notebook to production: Extract the notebook into versioned modules, define data contracts, package dependencies in a container, create reproducible training and evaluation pipeline steps, log metrics/artifacts, register the model only if it passes gates, and deploy through a controlled serving path. Add tests, monitoring, rollback, ownership, and documentation so the result is not just a notebook result but an operable production system.
MLOps situation - flaky retraining pipel: Collect failure patterns first: step, error type, data version, infrastructure state, time, and retry outcome. Add idempotent steps, retries for transient failures, stronger data validation, explicit resource requests, timeouts, checkpointing, and clearer error messages. Remove manual reruns by making rerun behavior safe and automated, and track pipeline success rate as an SLO for the ML platform.
MLOps situation - canary disagreement: T: Pause expansion and segment the support tickets by user type, request pattern, geography, model output, and canary/control group. Technical metrics may miss user-facing quality issues, so compare qualitative examples, business outcomes, confidence, and edge cases. If the tickets correlate with the candidate model, roll back or narrow the canary and add a release gate for that failure mode.
MLOps situation - compliance audit: An a: The platform should show the model version, artifact checksum, training data snapshot, feature values or feature snapshot used at inference time, code commit, pipeline run, parameters, approval record, deployment history, input/output logs where legally allowed, and monitoring status at that time. This requires lineage and retention policies designed before the audit, not reconstructed manually afterward.
MLOps situation - multi-team platform co: Separate experimentation and production paths. Give teams fast self-service sandboxes with quotas, templates, and lightweight governance, while production promotion requires stronger gates, approvals, lineage, security scans, and monitoring. The platform should make the easy path appropriate to the risk level instead of forcing every workflow through the same heavy process.
MLOps situation - stale features: Online: Detect stale features with freshness SLAs, timestamps, missing/update-rate checks, and alerts on feature-store lag, not just endpoint health. Mitigate by falling back to safe defaults, older champion model, cached validated features, or manual review depending on risk. Redesign with feature ownership, freshness dashboards, backfill/replay jobs, and serving logic that refuses unsafe stale inputs when required.
MLOps situation - disaster recovery: The: Serving should continue from already-loaded model replicas if possible, while new deployments are paused. For recovery, use replicated artifact storage, backup registry metadata, tested restore procedures, and a secondary region with known-good model versions. After failover, verify artifact checksums, model versions, traffic routing, and monitoring before resuming normal promotion workflows.
MLOps situation - interview unknown: You: Be honest about your hands-on level, then map the tool to concepts you do know: orchestration, tracking, registry, serving, monitoring, IAM, scaling, and rollback. Explain how you would evaluate it, what production questions you would ask, and how similar tools behave. A strong answer is not pretending; it is showing that your engineering model transfers across tools.
Multi-tenant AI platform: How would you: Issue each team a scoped API key routed through an internal gateway that enforces per-tenant rate limits and token budgets, and tag every request with a tenant identifier so cost and usage can be attributed accurately in billing dashboards. Isolate tenant data (prompts, retrieved context, logs) so one team cannot see another's traffic, and give each team visibility into their own quota consumption so they can self-manage before hitting a hard limit.
Resume deep-dive - Vertex AI Pipelines:: A typical pipeline starts with a data validation and preprocessing component, feeds into a training component that logs metrics and artifacts to MLflow or Vertex Experiments, then a conditional evaluation step that only proceeds to registration if the new model beats the current production model on defined metrics. The approved model is registered in Vertex AI Model Registry with a version and metadata, then deployed to an endpoint via a canary or staged rollout, with the whole pipeline defined declaratively (Kubeflow Pipelines SDK) so it's reproducible and re-runnable on new data.
Resume deep-dive - LLMOps stack: You dep: The choice depends on the use case: Ollama is convenient for quick local/dev experimentation and lower-throughput internal tools, while vLLM is the production serving choice for high-concurrency workloads because of its continuous batching and PagedAttention memory management, which dramatically improves throughput per GPU. Model choice (Llama 3 vs Mistral) came down to task performance on internal evals versus latency/cost tradeoffs at a given size. Scaling concurrent users meant horizontal pod autoscaling on GPU node pools, request queuing with backpressure so the service degrades gracefully rather than falling over, and continuous batching in vLLM to serve many concurrent requests per GPU efficiently.
Resume deep-dive - RAG pipeline: Walk me: The pipeline chunks source documents with an overlap strategy (typically 200-500 tokens per chunk with some overlap to preserve context across boundaries), embeds chunks with a domain-appropriate embedding model, and stores vectors with metadata for filtering. At query time it embeds the user question, retrieves the top-k most similar chunks, and passes them as grounding context to the LLM. Answer quality was measured with a mix of retrieval metrics (recall@k against a labeled set) and generation metrics (groundedness/faithfulness checks, either via an LLM-as-judge or human review) rather than relying on generation quality alone.
Resume deep-dive - model monitoring: You: Set drift thresholds based on statistical significance and a minimum sample size rather than reacting to any single alert, and required drift to persist across multiple consecutive monitoring windows before triggering a retrain, so a temporary traffic anomaly wouldn't cause an unnecessary retrain. Retraining was also gated by an evaluation step - the new model only replaced the production model if it demonstrably improved on held-out metrics, so even an unnecessary retrain couldn't silently degrade production.
Resume deep-dive - secure MLOps: You imp: Binary Authorization is a good example: it required every container image deployed to the ML serving cluster to carry a valid attestation from a vulnerability scan and CI pipeline signature before GKE would allow it to run, closing the risk of an unscanned or manually-pushed image (potentially with vulnerabilities or unauthorized code) reaching production. This meant no one, including someone with cluster access, could bypass the CI/CD pipeline and deploy an unverified image directly.
Resume deep-dive - GitOps for AI: You us: The Helm chart and Git state track not just application code but the model artifact reference (a versioned URI to the model in a registry or bucket), so a 'deployment' can mean either a code change or a model swap, and both need the same review and rollback discipline. Model-serving rollouts also need canary validation against model-specific metrics (accuracy, latency per token, GPU utilization) rather than just HTTP error rate, and rollback needs to revert both the serving code and the model version together, since deploying new code against an old model or vice versa can silently break behavior.
Scenario - hallucinated compliance answe: Pull the exact prompt, retrieved context, and model version from request logs to see whether the failure was a retrieval gap (correct source existed but wasn't surfaced), a stale/incorrect source document, or the model contradicting correct retrieved context - each has a different fix. Immediately add a disclaimer and human-escalation path for regulatory/compliance-adjacent topics if one didn't already exist, then add that exact failure case to your evaluation regression suite so a prompt or model change can't silently reintroduce it, and consider a stricter policy (retrieval-grounded-only answers, or routing compliance topics to a human) for high-risk categories rather than trusting general-purpose generation.
Scenario - runaway agent loop: An autono: Add hard limits first: a max iteration/tool-call count per task, a per-task and per-hour token/cost budget that kills the run when exceeded, and a timeout on the overall task, since none of these should have allowed an unattended loop to run all night. For detection, add real-time alerting on cost-rate and call-rate anomalies (not just a daily billing report) so a runaway loop pages someone within minutes, and add loop-detection logic that flags when the agent repeats near-identical tool calls without new progress, so it fails fast and safe instead of retrying indefinitely.
